description: Figure 1 presents a side-by-side comparison of optical micrographs illustrating the effect of processing route on the microstructure of lightweight high-manganese austenitic steels. Panel (a) is a bright-field optical micrograph of as-cast Fe-28Mn-3Al-0.9Si-1.2C-0.6Mo steel, revealing large primary austenite dendrites on the millimeter scale within a fully austenitic matrix. Panel (b) shows a differential interference contrast image of hot-rolled Fe-29Mn-6Al-0.9C-0.29Si-0.44Nb-0.44Mo steel, displaying fine equiaxed austenite grains with an average size of 50 μm, abundant annealing twins, and NbC particles pinning grain boundaries. Both specimens were etched with 10% nital. The figure demonstrates that hot rolling profoundly refines the grain structure compared with the as-cast condition, transforming coarse dendritic grains into fine, equiaxed, recrystallized austenite. This microstructural contrast establishes the foundational difference between cast and wrought lightweight steels, supporting the observation that wrought processing yields much smaller grain sizes and distinct boundary populations that influence subsequent mechanical behavior.

Figure 2 presents two age-hardening scatter plots showing Brinell hardness number (BHN) versus aging time in hours at 530°C for austenitic Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C lightweight steels. Panel (a) displays curves for low-phosphorus (<0.003% P) steels with varying silicon contents: 0.59% Si (open circles), 1.07% Si (open squares), and 1.56% Si (open diamonds). The caption states that increasing Si from 0.59% to 1.56% had no effect on solution-treated hardness but increased hardness during aging without significantly increasing the peak hardness. Panel (b) shows steels containing 1% Si with phosphorus levels of 0.001% (open circles), 0.006% (open squares), 0.018% (open diamonds), and 0.043% (open triangles). The visual data and caption together demonstrate that phosphorus greatly increases both the hardening rate and the peak aged hardness compared with silicon. Overall, the figure supports the core conclusion that both Si and P promote age hardening, but phosphorus exerts a far more pronounced effect on hardening kinetics and maximum hardness in these high-manganese steels.

Figure 3 presents paired dark-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images with selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) insets, revealing nanoscale precipitate distributions in Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels containing different silicon levels after identical thermal exposures. Panel (a) shows the 0.59% Si steel and panel (b) shows the 1.56% Si steel, both aged at 490°C for 63 h as stated in the caption. The bright nanoscale features dispersed throughout the dark austenitic matrix are identified by the caption as j-carbides (κ-carbides). Visual inspection and the caption both indicate that increasing silicon from 0.59% to 1.56% results in a noticeably higher number density and larger size of these carbide precipitates. The SAED insets provide corresponding crystallographic information, although individual diffraction spot indices remain unreadable at the given image resolution. This direct microstructural evidence supports the core finding that silicon accelerates κ-carbide formation and increases its volume fraction, which the citing text reports raises the carbon partitioning coefficient and the constrained austenite-κ-carbide mismatch to produce enhanced age hardening.

This figure presents two dark-field TEM images with corresponding SAED patterns (insets) comparing κ-carbide precipitation in Fe-30Mn-9Al-1Si-0.9C-0.5Mo steels with different phosphorus contents after aging for 0.5 h at 570°C. Panel (a) shows the 0.001% P steel, exhibiting a high number density of very small ordered domains only a few unit cells in dimension, with diffraction intensity corresponding to the perovskite crystal structure of κ-carbide; the scale bar is 10 nm. Panel (b) shows the 0.043% P steel, where the ordered domains have coarsened to an average size of 5 nm and exhibit preferential alignment along <100>; the scale bar is 25 nm. The visual comparison demonstrates that increasing phosphorus significantly increases both the size and number density of κ-carbides. These micrographs support the core conclusion that phosphorus accelerates spinodal decomposition and the ordering reaction during aging, promoting κ-carbide precipitation, yet caption states that phosphorus is randomly distributed between the austenite matrix and κ-carbide rather than partitioning via long-range diffusion.

Figure 5 presents paired atom probe tomography (APT) carbon concentration distribution maps showing the progress of spinodal decomposition in Fe-Mn-Al-C steels aged for 10 h at 530 °C. Subfigure (a) corresponds to a low-phosphorus steel containing 0.006% P, while subfigure (b) corresponds to a higher-phosphorus steel containing 0.043% P. The color scale ranges from 2 at.% C (blue) to 6 at.% C (red), with spatial axes given in nanometers. Visually, subfigure (a) exhibits a relatively homogeneous, predominantly blue-green carbon distribution with weak compositional fluctuations, whereas subfigure (b) displays more pronounced carbon-rich regions (yellow to red) interspersed with carbon-depleted zones (blue), indicating more advanced phase separation in the higher-phosphorus specimen. The caption and accompanying text state that phosphorus accelerates spinodal decomposition and increases the size and volume fraction of κ-carbide precipitation, providing direct nanoscale chemical evidence that elevated phosphorus enhances decomposition kinetics during aging, which correlates with the observed reductions in dynamic fracture toughness and Charpy V-notch impact energy in high-phosphorus variants.

Optical micrograph captured using differential interference contrast (DIC) of the hot-rolled Fe-30Mn-9Al-1.01Si-0.9C-0.5Mo lightweight steel in the peak-aged condition (60 h at 530°C), etched with nital. The image reveals an equiaxed austenitic grain structure with an average grain size of approximately 10–20 μm. Prominent dark grain boundary etching is visible across the field of view, which the caption attributes to preferential precipitation of κ-carbide (denoted as j-carbide) along austenite grain boundaries. Banding resulting from alloy segregation is also observable. The micrograph provides direct visual evidence of the microstructural degradation mechanism during peak aging: the associated text reports that this grain boundary carbide precipitation increases tensile strength by approximately 200 MPa but drastically lowers elongation from 54% to 14% compared with the solution-treated wrought condition. The 20 μm scale bar confirms the fine-grained microstructure produced by hot rolling at 900°C to an 84% reduction, solution treatment at 900°C for 10 min, and water quenching.

Figure 7 comprises two scatter plots comparing age-hardening tensile behavior of cast versus wrought Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steel. Subfigure (a) plots Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) in MPa versus aging time in hours on a logarithmic scale. Blue squares denote the wrought alloy and red circles denote the cast alloy. The wrought material shows UTS rising from approximately 950 MPa in the solution-treated condition (0.1 h) to about 1160 MPa after 50–60 h of aging, whereas the cast alloy reaches roughly 1000 MPa at 50 h and about 770 MPa at 20 h. Subfigure (b) plots UTS against Percent Elongation to Failure, illustrating a strength-ductility trade-off: at equivalent UTS levels, the wrought alloy exhibits higher elongation than the cast material, but peak aging shifts the wrought data toward lower ductility. The caption states that grain refinement from hot rolling produced the strength increase in the wrought alloy, and the accompanying citation reports that peak aging induced κ-carbide precipitation on grain boundaries, lowering elongation from 54% to 14% while increasing strength by roughly 200 MPa.

Figure 8 comprises three scanning electron micrographs of Charpy V-notch fracture surfaces from as-solution-treated and quenched Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels, comparing specimens containing 0.001% P versus 0.07% P. Subfigure (a) exhibits ductile tearing morphology with dimpled rupture and tear ridges at 20 μm scale for the low-phosphorus material. Subfigures (b) and (c) show the high-phosphorus material at 20 μm and 100 μm scales, respectively, revealing a low-energy cleavage-like fracture with river patterns, faceted steps, and a macroscopic dendritic pattern. The marked transition from ductile dimpled failure to brittle cleavage-like fracture demonstrates the catastrophic embrittling effect of phosphorus. These images support the core finding that increasing phosphorus from 0.001% to 0.07% drastically reduces notch toughness and changes the fracture mode, consistent with reported Charpy energy drops from 200 J to 28 J. The caption and text associate the dendritic pattern in the high-P specimens with fracture along {001} planes facilitated by phosphorus short-range order lowering cleavage strength.

This dual-axis plot presents the room-temperature Charpy V-notch impact energy and Brinell hardness as functions of phosphorus content for age-hardened Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels. The left vertical axis and black circular markers denote impact energy in J/cm², while the right vertical axis and black square markers represent Brinell Hardness Number (HBW). The horizontal axis shows phosphorus content ranging from 0 to 0.05 wt.%. The steels were aged at 530°C for 10 h, corresponding to the Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C base composition with approximately 1 wt.% Si. The data reveal a strong inverse correlation: as phosphorus increases from near 0 to approximately 0.045 wt.%, impact energy drops sharply from about 138 J/cm² to roughly 25 J/cm², while hardness concurrently rises from about 290 to 340 HBW. This figure demonstrates that phosphorus is highly detrimental to notch toughness, reducing it by over 80%, even though it marginally increases aged hardness. The caption states this behavior reflects phosphorus segregation during solidification and promotion of κ-carbide precipitation along austenite grain boundaries.

Figure 10 comprises two subplots linking inclusion populations to impact toughness in a 0.001% P Fe-Mn-Al-C steel. Subplot (a) is a bar chart of inclusion-type percentages in a calcium-treated, argon-stirred alloy, showing AlN as the dominant inclusion at roughly 26%, followed by pores (~17%), Mn-AlO (~14%), and AlN-MnO (~12%), with lesser amounts of Al2O3, MnS, Mn-Si-O, other nitrides, other oxides, and oxi-sulfides. Subplot (b) plots CVN energy (Joules) versus AlN density (particles/mm²) for five laboratory heats (base; Ca; Ca+Ce; Ca+Ce+Ar; Ca+Ce+Ar+teapot) and an industrial heat, revealing a strong inverse trend: CVN energy falls from about 38–39 J at low AlN densities (~3–4 particles/mm²) to near 19 J at the highest measured density (~49 particles/mm²). Heats with the full Ca+Ce+Ar+teapot treatment and the industrial heat cluster at low AlN density and high toughness, whereas the base, Ca-only, and Ca+Ce heats occupy higher-density, lower-energy regimes. The figure supports the conclusion that elevated AlN density is strongly deleterious to CVN impact energy and that argon stirring after calcium and misch metal treatment is effective in reducing AlN inclusions.

Figure 11 comprises four Charpy V-notch (CVN) impact energy versus testing temperature curves for aged Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C steels. Panel (a) shows 1.07% Si aged 12 h at 530°C (red circles), (b) shows 1.56% Si aged 12 h (blue diamonds), (c) shows 1.07% Si aged 30 h, and (d) shows 1.56% Si aged 30 h. All four plots display a ductile-to-brittle transition where energy decreases with decreasing temperature. At comparable aging times, the higher silicon level consistently yields lower impact energies across the entire temperature range; for example, at room temperature the 12 h specimens drop from approximately 92 J to 68 J when Si increases from 1.07% to 1.56%, while the 30 h specimens drop from about 58 J to 35 J. Extending aging from 12 h to 30 h further reduces toughness at all temperatures for both Si contents. The caption states that increased j-carbide precipitation is responsible for the marked decrease in impact energy. Together, the subfigures demonstrate that both higher silicon additions and prolonged aging at 530°C progressively degrade the notch toughness and elevate the ductile-to-brittle transition behavior in these lightweight austenitic steels.

Figure 12 consists of two scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs displaying the fracture surfaces of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C lightweight austenitic steels with different silicon contents after aging and cryogenic impact testing. Subfigure (a) shows the 1.07% Si specimen and (b) shows the 1.56% Si specimen, both aged for 12 h at 530-C and broken at -40-C as stated in the caption. Both images reveal predominantly transgranular cleavage-like fracture morphology at the 20 μm scale, with distinct slip traces visible on certain favorably oriented cleavage facets. The micrographs demonstrate that increasing silicon content does not change the primary brittle fracture mode at low temperature but correlates with reduced notch toughness, consistent with reported Charpy energy decreases from 92 J to 68 J at room temperature and an average 15 J reduction at -40-C. The observed slip traces support the proposed slipping-off mechanism via glide plane decohesion along {111} planes associated with planar slip and k-carbide precipitation during aging. Together, these images provide direct morphological evidence that silicon additions promote brittle transgranular cleavage fracture in aged high-Mn steels under cryogenic conditions.

Figure 13 presents a pair of scanning electron microscopy fractographs showing the Charpy V-notch fracture surfaces of Fe-30Mn-9Al-2.7Si-0.9C steel specimens under two processing conditions, with a 20 μm scale bar in each. Subfigure (a) depicts the as-hot-rolled specimen, where the surface consists mainly of transgranular cleavage facets bearing {111} slip traces, indicating brittle fracture attributed to κ-carbide precipitation and short-range order developed during slow cooling after deformation. Subfigure (b) shows the identical alloy after solution treatment for 10 min at 1100 °C and water quenching, exhibiting a completely ductile fracture morphology characterized by extensive microvoid coalescence and dimpled rupture. The stark contrast between brittle cleavage in the as-hot-rolled state and fully ductile failure after solution treatment visually confirms the dramatic improvement in notch toughness— from roughly 23 J to about 160 J breaking energy—reported in the text. These micrographs support the core conclusion that dissolving deleterious precipitates via solution treatment is essential for achieving high toughness in lightweight high-manganese-aluminum steels.

Figure 14 presents four scanning electron micrographs of dynamic fracture toughness (DFT) specimen fracture surfaces from Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C steels aged at 530°C, comparing the effects of silicon content (1.07% Si in a and c; 1.56% Si in b and d) and aging hardness (approximately 300 BHN in a and b; 350 BHN in c and d). The micrographs reveal a progressive transition from ductile failure, characterized by dimpled rupture, to brittle transgranular cleavage-type fracture with increasing age hardening and silicon content. At the lower hardness of ~300 BHN, the 1.07% Si specimen exhibits more ductile features, while the 1.56% Si counterpart shows a mixed mode; at ~350 BHN, both silicon levels display pronounced cleavage facets. The caption notes that {111} slip traces are present on favorably oriented cleavage facets in the higher-hardness panels (c and d), suggesting fracture by decohesion of glide planes. Collectively, these images provide direct fractographic evidence that increased silicon and aging hardness promote brittle cleavage mechanisms, correlating with reduced dynamic fracture toughness in these lightweight austenitic high-manganese steels.

This figure presents automated inclusion characterization data for cast Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C lightweight steels containing three silicon levels—0.59%, 1.07%, and 1.56% Si—shown as two grouped bar charts. Subfigure (a) plots average inclusion density in Particles/mm² by inclusion type. The total inclusion density is reported to be similar across the three low-phosphorus steels (62–78 inclusions/mm²), and the bar heights confirm comparable populations for AlN, Al-N-Mn-O, Al-N-Mn-S, Mn-Al-O, MnO, and MnS. The sole major divergence is in micro-defects (porosity): the 0.59% Si steel shows roughly 80 particles/mm², more than twice the value for the 1.07% Si steel (~33 particles/mm²) and nearly double that of the 1.56% Si steel (~47 particles/mm²). Subfigure (b) displays average inclusion size in microns, where the bars indicate largely similar dimensions across the three compositions for each inclusion category, with no systematic silicon-dependent trend. Together, the charts support the conclusion that the inferior notch toughness of the aged 0.59% Si steel arises primarily from its substantially higher porosity rather than from differences in inclusion type, overall density, or average inclusion size.
